assessment_history.summary: The PRD for "Skafolding" describes a Go service scaffold using the Echo framework with a ping endpoint and Kubernetes-compatible health/uptime endpoints. The core intent is reasonably clear, but the document is essentially a single loosely structured paragraph — it lacks formal sections for Goals, Non-Goals, and Background. Additionally, several implementation details are ambiguous or missing (e.g., exact Kubernetes probe endpoints, security middleware specifics, and deployment context). The PRD can proceed after a few targeted clarifications.
gaps: No formal 'Goals' section with measurable outcomes (e.g., response time targets, availability SLOs, test coverage expectations).
No 'Non-Goals' section — scope boundaries are entirely absent, leaving it unclear what is intentionally excluded (e.g., authentication, database integration, deployment manifests).
No 'Background' section — there is no context about why this scaffold is being created, what problem it solves, or what prior art or existing services informed the design.
The 'Intent' is implicit rather than explicitly stated; it is inferable but not formally articulated.
'Typical health and uptime monitoring endpoints' is vague — specific routes (e.g., /healthz, /readyz, /livez, /metrics) are not enumerated.

The Echo framework 'typical handlers for logging, error handling and security' are not specified — it is unclear which middleware stack is expected (e.g., zerolog, recover, CORS, Helmet, rate-limiting).
Owner field is empty — no accountable party is identified for this spec.
'Reusable code in dedicated root folders' is mentioned with examples but no concrete packages are defined for this service.
No mention of Go version, module path/name, or any configuration management approach (env vars, config files, etc.).

questions.text: What is the explicit, one-sentence intent of this scaffold? Is the goal to provide a reusable starting template for all future Go services, or is this a specific standalone service?
questions.context: The Intent section must be clearly and formally articulated. Understanding whether this is a reusable template vs. a one-off service changes the scope and design significantly.
options: Reusable scaffold/template for all future Go microservices in this repo/org
A specific standalone Go service that happens to start minimal
Both — a working service that also serves as a reference implementation

questions.text: Which specific Kubernetes health/uptime endpoints should the service expose?
questions.context: Kubernetes probes require specific routes. The PRD says 'typical' endpoints but does not enumerate them, which is insufficient for code generation.

questions.text: Which Echo middleware should be included in the default middleware stack?
questions.context: The PRD mentions 'typical handlers for logging, error handling and security' without specifying which ones. This is needed to generate the correct scaffolding code.

questions.text: What are the explicit Non-Goals for this scaffold? What should it deliberately NOT include?
questions.context: Non-Goals prevent scope creep and clarify what artifact generators should omit. For example: no auth, no database, no deployment YAML, no business logic.

questions.text: What is the background or motivation for creating this scaffold? Is there an existing service or template it replaces, or is this greenfield?
questions.context: The Background section provides context for why this work is being done. This helps reviewers and future maintainers understand the rationale.
options: Replacing an existing template or boilerplate
Standardizing across multiple teams/repos that currently diverge

questions.text: What Go module path and Go version should be used for this service?
questions.context: Code generation requires a concrete module name (e.g., github.com/org/skafolding) and a target Go version (e.g., 1.22).

questions.text: Should the scaffold include configuration management (e.g., environment variable parsing, a config struct)? If so, which library, if any, is preferred?
questions.context: Most production Go services need some form of config loading. It is unclear if this is in or out of scope for the initial scaffold.
options: Yes — env vars only (e.g., os.Getenv or godotenv)
Yes — structured config with a library (e.g., viper, envconfig, koanf)
No — keep it out of scope for now

assessment_history.summary: The PRD is well-structured, clearly scoped, and covers the core requirements for a minimal Go scaffold service. Goals, non-goals, endpoints, middleware, and project layout are all defined. However, several gaps remain that could lead to ambiguity or inconsistency during implementation — particularly around response contracts, probe semantics, port configurability, Go version accuracy, testing expectations, and README scope.
gaps: Response bodies for /healthz and /readyz are not specified — it is unclear whether they return JSON, plain text, or an empty body.

The semantic distinction between /healthz (liveness) and /readyz (readiness) is not elaborated. Since there is no real dependency (no DB, no cache), it is unclear whether both endpoints behave identically or whether /readyz is intended to carry future-ready logic.
Go 1.25 does not exist as of the latest stable release (Go 1.24 is current). This should be verified or corrected to avoid go.mod toolchain issues.

The hardcoded default server address (':8080') is mentioned in passing but not formally specified — no acceptance criterion validates the port, and no mention of whether the port should be documented in the README.
No testing requirements are stated — it is ambiguous whether unit tests or integration tests are expected as part of this scaffold, or if testing is entirely out of scope.
The README.md file is listed in the project layout but its required content is not defined — expected sections (e.g., how to run, how to build, endpoint list) are unspecified.
The /ping endpoint response body is given as an example (e.g., {"message":"pong"}) but not formally mandated — the Content-Type header (application/json) is also not explicitly required.
CORS middleware is configured with 'Echo's default CORS configuration' but the defaults are not spelled out, which may cause inconsistency if a developer uses a non-default Echo version.
No linting, formatting, or code style requirements are stated (e.g., gofmt, golangci-lint), which is common for greenfield Go projects establishing conventions.

questions.text: What should /healthz and /readyz return as a response body?
questions.context: The PRD only specifies '200 OK' for both probe endpoints. Defining the response body (and Content-Type) ensures consistent implementation and makes acceptance criteria testable.

questions.text: Should /healthz and /readyz behave identically in this initial scaffold, or should /readyz contain a stub that could be extended later (e.g., a placeholder comment for dependency checks)?
questions.context: Since there are no real dependencies, both probes could be identical. However, a stub or comment in /readyz would signal intent and make it easier to extend later without changing the file structure.
options: Both endpoints are identical — return 200 OK with no distinction
Both return 200 OK, but /readyz should include a comment indicating where dependency checks would be added

questions.text: Go 1.25 does not currently exist (latest stable is Go 1.24). Which Go version should be targeted in go.mod?
questions.context: Using a non-existent Go version in go.mod will cause toolchain errors. Clarifying the intended version prevents build failures.
options: Go 1.25 (anticipating a future release — use as-is)

questions.text: Should the server's listening address/port be documented in the README, and is ':8080' the only intended default?
questions.context: The PRD mentions ':8080' as a hardcoded default but does not formally require it to be documented or tested. Clarifying this ensures the README and acceptance criteria are aligned.
options: Yes — document ':8080' in the README as the default
No — port documentation is out of scope for this scaffold
Yes — and add an acceptance criterion that the server starts on :8080

questions.text: Are any tests (unit or integration) required as part of this scaffold?
questions.context: The PRD has no mention of testing. For a greenfield scaffold, even a single smoke test can establish the pattern. Knowing whether tests are in scope affects the project layout and acceptance criteria.
options: No tests required — testing is explicitly out of scope
At least one example unit test should be included (e.g., for the ping handler)
A basic integration/smoke test using httptest should be included

questions.text: What content should the README.md include?
questions.context: The README is listed in the project layout but its content is undefined. For a starter scaffold, the README is often the first thing a new developer reads.
options: Minimal: project name, how to build, how to run
Standard: project name, prerequisites, build, run, endpoint list, project structure overview
Detailed: all of the above plus contributing guidelines and middleware notes
README content is out of scope — an empty or placeholder file is acceptable

questions.text: Should the /ping response body and Content-Type be formally mandated, or remain as a suggested example?
questions.context: The current wording uses 'e.g.' which leaves the response format open to interpretation. Mandating the exact body and Content-Type (application/json) makes the acceptance criteria unambiguous and testable.
options: Keep as a suggested example — any valid JSON body is acceptable
Plain text 'pong' is also acceptable (no JSON required)

questions.text: Should any code style or linting standards be enforced as part of this scaffold (e.g., gofmt, golangci-lint)?
questions.context: Greenfield projects often establish linting and formatting conventions from the start. Including or explicitly excluding these sets expectations for contributors.
options: No linting requirements — gofmt compliance is sufficient and implied
gofmt must pass (add to acceptance criteria)
golangci-lint with a default config should be included

answers.q1: Just a starter service to get the ball rolling.
answers.q7: No, keep it as simple as possible for now.
